title: TypeScript Error Detective
description: A production TypeScript app is failing in CI. Sixteen bugs, sixteen concepts — from basic type annotations to advanced generics and discriminated unions. Fix every error and master the type system.

content.scenario: CI reports: 'Argument of type number is not assignable to parameter of type string'. A teammate pushed this user-greeting function. The ID should display as text, but they passed the raw numeric ID.
content.errorMessage: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'
content.hint: Look at what formatWelcome expects vs. what you're passing. Which property of user is a string?

content.keyInsight: Type annotations are compile-time contracts. Every type error caught by the compiler is a runtime bug that will never reach production.

interactiveDemo.prompt: Fix each variable assignment to match its type annotation

content.scenario: The user profile page crashes: 'Property phone does not exist on type User'. The designer added a phone field to the UI, but nobody updated the TypeScript interface. The API sometimes returns phone, sometimes doesn't.
content.errorMessage: Property 'phone' does not exist on type 'User'
content.hint: The API sometimes returns phone, sometimes doesn't. TypeScript has syntax for properties that may or may not exist.

content.keyInsight: Interfaces define object shapes. Optional properties (?) model real-world data where fields may be missing. TypeScript uses structural typing — shape matters, not class name.

expandableSections.title: Interface vs Type alias — when to use which
readonly — immutability at the type level

steps.title: The API Returns Two Different Shapes

content.scenario: Your API returns `{ status: 'success', data: User }` or `{ status: 'error', message: string }`. The current code treats them the same and crashes when trying to access `.data` on error responses. Make the types distinguish success from error.
content.hint: Instead of one interface with optional fields, create separate interfaces for success and error — each with a literal string for status. Then combine them with |.

steps.title: Discriminated Unions — The Most Powerful Pattern

content.keyInsight: Discriminated unions combine literal types + union types + type narrowing. They model shapes that vary by state — API responses, events, actions. Check the tag, and TypeScript knows the exact shape.

expandableSections.title: Exhaustiveness checking with never
How this relates to algebraic data types
expandableSections.content: In type theory, discriminated unions are **tagged unions** or **sum types** — the type-level equivalent of logical OR. They appear in every typed functional language: Haskell's `data`, Rust's `enum`, OCaml's variants. TypeScript's version is structural (no explicit declaration needed), making it uniquely flexible.

content.scenario: This function accepts string | number but TypeScript won't let you call .toUpperCase() on it. The if-check you wrote doesn't narrow the type correctly. Fix the type guard.
content.errorMessage: Property 'toUpperCase' does not exist on type 'number'
content.hint: Truthiness checks don't narrow types (0 and '' are falsy but still valid). You need typeof to distinguish string from number.

steps.title: Type Narrowing — How TypeScript Reads Your Code

content.keyInsight: TypeScript narrows types by analyzing your control flow. typeof, instanceof, in, and custom type guards each narrow differently. Truthiness checks only remove null/undefined.

expandableSections.title: Assertion functions

content.scenario: You wrote a function to safely get the first element of any array. But TypeScript loses the type — it returns unknown. The calling code needs a number, gets unknown, and fails.
content.errorMessage: Type 'unknown' is not assignable to type 'number'
content.hint: What if the function could 'remember' the type of array it receives? TypeScript has a way to parameterize functions with types.

content.keyInsight: Generics are type-level variables — they parameterize functions and types while preserving exact type information. They are the foundation of reusable, type-safe code.

expandableSections.title: Multiple type parameters
Generics in interfaces and classes

content.scenario: Your fetch wrapper returns data, but TypeScript says the response body is 'any' — no type safety on the API response. Callers can access any property without errors. Type the function so callers get real types back.
content.errorMessage: No type safety on fetch response
content.hint: Combine generics with async/await. The caller should specify what type the JSON represents: fetchJson<User>('/api/user/1')

content.keyInsight: Async functions return Promise<T>. Since fetch().json() returns any, you need generics or assertions to restore type safety. Combine with discriminated unions for type-safe error handling.

expandableSections.title: Runtime validation with Zod

content.scenario: Your update function requires all User fields, but PATCH requests should accept partial updates. Someone who only wants to change their email shouldn't need to send name, phone, etc.
content.hint: TypeScript has built-in utility types that transform existing types. One makes all properties optional.

content.keyInsight: Utility types transform existing types without rewriting. Partial, Pick, Omit, Record cover 90% of real-world type transformations. They compose: Partial<Omit<User, 'id'>>.

expandableSections.title: Building custom utility types
Extract and Exclude — type-level filtering

content.scenario: Combine everything. This e-commerce cart function has 3 type errors. Fix all of them: the discriminated union handling, the utility type for the update, and the generic return type.
content.hint: Bug 1: Check result.status before accessing .items. Bug 2: Use Partial<Product>. Bug 3: Make findProduct generic.

content.theory:
## The Complete Picture

You've fixed 8 bugs and discovered 7 concepts. Here's how they connect:

```
Type Annotations
  define contracts for function parameters, return values

Interfaces
  define contracts for object shapes
  optional (?) and readonly modifiers

Union Types (A | B)
  model values that can be one of several types

Discriminated Unions
  union types with a literal tag property
  TypeScript narrows automatically on the tag

Type Narrowing
  typeof, instanceof, in, custom type guards
  control flow analysis in if/switch

Generics (<T>)
  type-level variables
  preserve type info in reusable code
  constraints via 'extends'

Utility Types
  Partial, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly
  transform types without rewriting
  built on mapped types (keyof + in)
```

### The Mental Shift

TypeScript is a **proof system**. Every type annotation is a logical proposition the compiler verifies. When your code compiles, you have a guarantee: shapes match, nulls are checked, branches are exhaustive.

### Where to Go Next

- **Conditional types**: `T extends U ? X : Y` — if-else at the type level
- **Template literal types**: `` type Route = `/api/${string}` `` — pattern-match strings
- **Infer keyword**: Extract types from complex structures
- **Module augmentation**: Extend third-party library types
content.keyInsight: TypeScript's type system is a proof system. Annotations, interfaces, unions, generics, and narrowing work together to guarantee correctness at compile time — before a single line runs.

expandableSections.title: The Curry-Howard correspondence
expandableSections.content: When you write `function add(a: number, b: number): number`, you state a theorem: 'given two numbers, this produces a number.' The compiler proves it by checking every code path. This connection between types-as-propositions and programs-as-proofs is the Curry-Howard correspondence — foundational to type theory.

expandableSections.title: TypeScript's intentional unsoundness
expandableSections.content:
TypeScript has known holes by design:

- `as` assertions bypass checking
- `any` opts out entirely
- Covariant arrays: `Dog[]` assignable to `Animal[]` (technically unsound)
- Index signatures don't guarantee key existence

These are intentional trade-offs for JavaScript compatibility. Strict mode + eslint catches most issues in practice.
